Object detection is a key component of many computer vision systems.
Training a robust object detector requires a large amount of training data with accurate bounding box annotations.
There has been increasing attention on few-shot object detection as a way to learn to detect novel object categories from just a few annotated training samples.
Few-shot object detection learns to detect novel object categories from just a few annotated training samples.
The FSOD method can handle different object crops of the same object instance.
State-of-the-art FSOD methods are built on top of a two-stage framework, including a region proposal network that generates multiple image crops from the input image.
The two-stage framework includes a classifier that labels the generated image proposals.
The region proposal network generalizes well to novel classes, but the classifier is more error-prone due to the lack of training data diversity.
To mitigate the issue of lack of training data diversity for novel classes, a natural approach is to generate additional features.
Zhang et al. propose a feature hallucination network to use the variation from base classes to diversify training data for novel classes.
Zhu et al. propose to synthesize visual features for unseen objects based on a conditional variational auto-encoder for zero-shot detection.
The lack of data diversity is still a challenging issue for FSOD methods.
Given a test image, the classifier needs to accurately classify multiple object proposals that overlap the object instance in various ways.
The features of object proposals exhibit great variability induced by different object scales, parts, positions, and backgrounds.
The features of image crops exhibit great variability induced by different object scales.
The features of image crops exhibit great variability induced by object parts included in the crops.
The features of image crops exhibit great variability induced by object positions within the crops.
The features of image crops exhibit great variability induced by backgrounds.
DeFRCN method only classifies correctly a few among many proposals overlapping an object instance of a few-shot class.
Different ways of cropping an object can result in features with various difficulty levels.

Building a classifier robust against crop-related variation is challenging.

A novel data generation method is proposed to mitigate the issue of hard cases.
Features with diverse crop-related variations are generated for the few-shot classes.
The generated features are used as additional training data to train the classifier.
The difficulty levels of the features vary from easy to hard with respect to how the object is cropped.
The generative model allows control of the difficulty levels of the generated samples.
Features are generated from a latent space.
The magnitude of the latent code linearly correlates with the difficulty level of the generated feature.
The latent code of a harder feature is placed further away from the origin.
The difficulty level can be controlled by changing the norm of the feature.
The difficulty level is controlled by changing the norm of the latent code. The magnitude of the latent code is associated with the difficulty level of the object proposal.

The data generation model is based on a VAE architecture, which consists of an encoder and a decoder. The encoder maps the input to a latent representation, and the decoder reconstructs the input from the latent code.
The inputs to the VAE are object proposal features extracted from a pre-trained object detector.

The norm of the latent code is rescaled to correlate linearly with the IoU score of the input object proposal. The IoU score indicates the overlap of the object proposal with the ground-truth object box.
The IoU score partially indicates the difficulty level, with a high score indicating significant overlap and a low score indicating a harder case.
The decoder is biased to generate harder samples by increasing the magnitude of the latent code.
The latent code magnitude is mentioned in relation to vice versa, suggesting a possible interdependency or correlation between the two concepts
Latent codes are used to obtain a diverse set of features for additional training data, indicating a direct relationship between latent codes and features
Features obtained from latent codes are used as additional training data for the few-shot classifier, showing a connection between features and the few-shot classifier
The VAE model is first trained using abundant data from the base classes, implying a relationship between the VAE model and the abundant data
The VAE model is conditioned on the semantic code of the input instance category, indicating a dependency between the VAE model and the semantic code
The semantic embedding of the few-shot class is used as the conditional code to synthesize new features, suggesting a relationship between semantic embedding and conditional code
The baseline few-shot object detector is fine-tuned using VAE-generated features, illustrating a connection between the baseline detector and VAE-generated features
A vanilla conditional VAE model trained with ground-truth box features brings improvement over the DeFRCN baseline, indicating a relationship between ground-truth box features and improvement
The proposed Norm-VAE can further improve the new state-of-the-art, suggesting a connection between Norm-VAE and the new state-of-the-art
Generated features consistently improve the state-of-the-art few-shot object detector, indicating a relationship between generated features and the few-shot object detector
Norm-VAE consistently improves the state-of-the-art few-shot object detector for both PASCAL VOC and MS COCO datasets.
Our experiments show that the object detectors trained with our additional features achieve state-of-the-art FSOD in both PASCAL VOC and MS COCO datasets.

edge: Our experiments show that the object detectors trained with our additional features achieve state-of-the-art FSOD in both PASCAL VOC and MS COCO datasets.
We show that lack of crop-related diversity in training data of novel classes is a crucial problem for FSOD.
We propose Norm-VAE, a novel VAE architecture that can effectively increase crop-related diversity in difficulty levels into the generated samples to support the training of FSOD classifiers.
Few-shot object detection aims to detect novel classes from limited annotated examples of previously unseen classes.
A number of prior methods have been proposed to address this challenging task of few-shot object detection.
One line of work focuses on the meta-learning paradigm, which has been widely explored in few-shot classification.
The prediction network is designed to predict novel classes.
Specialized meta-strategies are proposed to disentangle the learning of category-agnostic and category-specific components in a CNN based detection model.
A two-stage fine-tuning strategy has shown great potential recently.

Another line of work adopts a two-stage fine-tuning strategy and it has shown great potential recently. Proposal [42] suggests to fine-tune only box classifier and box regressor with novel data while freezing the other parameters of the model.
Proposal [42] suggests to fine-tune only box classifier and box regressor with novel data while freezing the other parameters of the model. This simple strategy outperforms previous meta-learners.

Two different models are compared based on their performance, where Vanilla-VAE denotes the model trained with generated features from a vanilla VAE, while Norm-VAE denotes the model trained with features generated from the proposed Norm-VAE model.
Using additional data from the proposed Norm-VAE model consistently improves the results across all settings, indicating a relationship between Norm-VAE and additional data.

Simply using a VAE model trained with the original data outperforms the state-of-the-art method DeFRCN in all shot and split on PASCAL VOC benchmark, suggesting a relationship between the VAE model and original data.

Our generated features in the Norm-VAE model bring significant improvements in most cases of FSOD results, especially in low-shot settings, indicating a relationship between Norm-VAE and FSOD results.
